title: Add algorithm sonification mode with Web Audio API
description:
Add a toggleable sound mode where array element accesses produce musical tones. Higher values = higher pitch. Comparisons play both tones simultaneously. Swaps play a distinct percussion hit. Sorted arrays produce an ascending scale.

Implement using Web Audio API OscillatorNode. Map array values to frequency range (200Hz-2000Hz). Add a speaker icon toggle in the playback controls section of AlgorithmPanel.tsx.

Reference: Sorting.at demonstrates that sonified sorting is highly viral and educational — users can HEAR the difference between O(n²) and O(n log n).

Integration point: In AlgorithmModule.tsx handleStepChange (line 947), when a new step arrives, parse mutations for 'comparing'/'swapping' states and trigger corresponding audio.
acceptanceCriteria: Speaker icon toggle in playback controls enables/disables sound
Each comparison plays two tones corresponding to compared values
Each swap plays a distinct percussive sound
Sorted state plays ascending scale confirmation
Sound disabled by default (opt-in)
Volume control or fixed reasonable level
Works in Chrome, Firefox, Safari
No sound when prefers-reduced-motion is active

title: Build algorithm race mode with 3-5 simultaneous visualizations
description:
Add a 'Race Mode' that runs 3-5 sorting algorithms side-by-side on the same input data simultaneously. Users see bars moving in parallel and viscerally experience the speed differences.

UI: Replace the single canvas with a grid of mini-ArrayVisualizers (3 columns or 2x2+1 layout). Each has its own PlaybackController. All share the same input array. A countdown '3-2-1-GO!' starts all simultaneously.

Add a race configuration panel where users select 3-5 algorithms from a checkbox list. Show a finish-order leaderboard when algorithms complete.

This is the interactive version of the viral YouTube 'sorting algorithm comparison' videos. Frontend-only, no backend needed.
acceptanceCriteria: User can select 3-5 sorting algorithms for the race
All algorithms run on identical input data simultaneously
Mini-visualizers show in a responsive grid layout
Countdown animation before race starts
Each visualizer shows algorithm name and step count
Finish order displayed as algorithms complete
Final leaderboard shows: algorithm name, total steps, comparisons, swaps
Race can be replayed with same or different data

title: Build custom graph editor with click-to-create and drag-to-connect
description:
Currently all graph algorithms run on pre-built sample graphs. Add an interactive graph editor where users can:
1. Click on empty canvas to create a node
2. Drag from one node to another to create an edge
3. Click an edge to set its weight
4. Toggle directed/undirected per edge
5. Delete nodes/edges via right-click or delete key
6. Import/export graph as JSON

Replace the static 'Graph Data' info box in AlgorithmPanel.tsx (lines 911-935) with a 'Use Sample' / 'Custom Graph' toggle. When 'Custom Graph' is active, the canvas becomes the editor. When Run is clicked, switch to visualization mode.

This enables interview candidates to test algorithms on their own problem graphs.
acceptanceCriteria: Click on canvas creates a new node
Drag between nodes creates an edge with weight input
Nodes and edges can be deleted
Directed/undirected toggle per edge
Graph can be exported as JSON and re-imported
Any graph algorithm can run on the custom graph
Sample graphs still available via quick-select buttons
Editor validates graph constraints (e.g., A* needs goal node)

title: Add live code panel with language toggle (TypeScript/Python/Java)
description:
Add a code panel that shows the actual implementation code for the selected algorithm, with the current line highlighted as the animation plays. Support toggling between TypeScript, Python, and Java.

Implement as a new panel section (tab or expandable) in the properties panel. Use a simple syntax highlighter (Prism.js or highlight.js — already likely in the bundle for code display).

Code content should be stored alongside each algorithm's config as a `code` field with language variants. The pseudocodeLine from AnimationStep maps to the actual code line via a mapping array.

This bridges the gap between 'I understand the visual' and 'I can write the code'.
acceptanceCriteria: Code panel shows implementation in selected language
Language toggle between TypeScript, Python, Java
Current execution line is highlighted during playback
Highlighting syncs with step-by-step navigation
Code is scrollable for long algorithms
Copy button copies code to clipboard
At least sorting algorithms have all 3 language versions

title: Add element journey tracker — click any bar to follow its full path
description:
When a user clicks on any bar in the ArrayVisualizer during a sorting visualization, highlight that specific element across ALL steps — showing its complete journey from initial position to final sorted position.

Display a small overlay: 'Element 7: started at index 3, moved to index 1 (swap), moved to index 0 (swap), final position: index 0. Total moves: 2.'

This builds deep intuition for HOW sorting algorithms work — students can see that in Quick Sort, the pivot stays still while others move around it, vs in Bubble Sort, large elements 'bubble' rightward step by step.

Implement by scanning all steps' mutations for the selected element's targetId, building a position history, and rendering a mini-timeline overlay.
acceptanceCriteria: Clicking any bar in ArrayVisualizer selects it for tracking
Selected bar has a distinct visual indicator (border/glow)
Overlay shows element value, starting position, all moves, final position
During playback, the tracked element has extra emphasis (pulsing glow)
Click elsewhere or press Escape to deselect
Works correctly for all 6 working sorting algorithms

title: Add presentation mode for lecture-hall use
description:
Add a full-screen presentation mode optimized for projectors and lecture halls:
1. Full-screen canvas with no sidebar chrome
2. Larger text sizes (1.5x-2x current)
3. Higher contrast colors
4. Keyboard-only control (Space=play, arrows=step, Esc=exit)
5. Algorithm name and step counter as subtle overlay
6. Current step description as large bottom banner

Activated via a 'Present' button (Maximize icon) in the module header or via keyboard shortcut (F11 or Cmd+Shift+F).

This serves professors who currently have to explain visualizations that are too small for the back row to see.
acceptanceCriteria: Present button enters full-screen mode via Fullscreen API
Canvas fills entire screen, sidebar hidden
Text sizes increased for visibility at distance
All playback controls work via keyboard
Algorithm name visible as overlay
Step description shown as large banner at bottom
Escape exits presentation mode cleanly
Works in all major browsers supporting Fullscreen API

title: Add guided learning path with progressive difficulty sequence
description:
The current flat dropdown of 66 algorithms is overwhelming for beginners. Add a 'Learning Path' mode that presents algorithms in a curated pedagogical sequence:

Path 1 — Sorting Fundamentals:
  Bubble Sort → Selection Sort → Insertion Sort → Merge Sort → Quick Sort → Heap Sort
  Each step: brief explanation of what's new, why it's better than the previous.

Path 2 — Graph Basics:
  BFS → DFS → Dijkstra → Bellman-Ford → Kruskal → Prim

Path 3 — Dynamic Programming:
  Fibonacci → Coin Change → Knapsack → LCS → Edit Distance

UI: Add a 'Learning Path' tab above the algorithm selector in AlgorithmPanel. Show current position in path, unlocked/locked state, 'Next' button. Store progress in localStorage.

This transforms a reference tool into a learning tool.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 3 learning paths with 5-6 algorithms each
Path UI shows current position and progress
Each algorithm in path has a brief 'what to notice' prompt
Next button advances to next algorithm with data pre-loaded
Progress persisted in localStorage
Can exit path mode and return to free exploration anytime
Completion of a path shows congratulatory message

title: Add adaptive speed — auto-slow on pivots/splits, auto-fast on boring passes
description:
Currently playback speed is constant. But some steps are educational (pivot selection, merge operation, rotation) while others are boring (comparing two already-sorted elements in a mostly-sorted array).

Add an 'Adaptive Speed' toggle that adjusts playback speed per step based on step type:
- Swap/pivot/split steps: use base speed (or slower)
- Simple comparison-only steps with no state change: 2-3x faster
- Sorted-marking steps: 2x faster

Implement by examining the step's mutations in PlaybackController.scheduleNext(). If all mutations are 'highlight' → 'comparing' (no swap, no state change), reduce the delay.

The step.duration field at AlgorithmModule.tsx types.ts:16 already exists — engines already set different durations (400ms for compare, 500ms for swap, 300ms for mark-sorted in bubble-sort.ts). The PlaybackController at line 117 already uses `currentStep.duration / this.speed`. So this may already work partially — verify at runtime.
acceptanceCriteria: Adaptive speed toggle in playback controls
Educational steps (swap, pivot, merge) play at base speed
Boring steps (comparison-only, marking) play 2-3x faster
Speed transitions are smooth (no jarring jumps)
Manual speed control still works alongside adaptive mode
Toggle persists within session

title: Add jargon tooltips for technical terms in algorithm descriptions
description:
Algorithm descriptions and properties use jargon that intimidates self-taught developers: 'stable', 'in-place', 'O(n log n)', 'pivot', 'MST', 'DAG', 'SCC'.

Add hover tooltips on technical terms that provide plain-English explanations:
- 'Stable' → 'Equal elements keep their original order'
- 'In-Place' → 'Sorts without needing extra memory for a copy'
- 'O(n²)' → 'Roughly n×n operations — doubles in time when data doubles'
- 'Pivot' → 'A chosen element that others are compared against'

Implement as a small component `<JargonTip term='stable'>Stable</JargonTip>` that wraps terms in the description and badge text. Use Radix UI Tooltip or a simple CSS-only tooltip.

Apply in AlgorithmPanel.tsx (lines 770-797 for badges) and AlgorithmModule.tsx (lines 313-355 for complexity section).
acceptanceCriteria: Technical terms in description have dotted underline indicating tooltip
Hovering shows plain-English explanation
Tooltips cover: stable, in-place, all Big-O notations, pivot, MST, SCC, DAG
Tooltips don't interfere with normal text selection
Works on touch devices via tap
No layout shift when tooltips appear

title: Add algorithm selection advisor — 'Which algorithm should I use?'
description:
Add an interactive decision helper that asks questions about the user's problem and recommends an algorithm:

Q1: What type of problem? (Sort / Search / Shortest Path / MST / ...)
Q2: Data characteristics? (Nearly sorted / Random / Contains duplicates / Negative weights / ...)
Q3: Constraints? (Memory limited / Need stability / Need worst-case guarantee / ...)

Based on answers, highlight recommended algorithms in the selector and show a brief explanation of why.

Implement as a small wizard/flowchart panel above the algorithm selector. Decision tree is hardcoded (no AI needed). Result: 1-3 recommended algorithms with 'Try it' buttons.

This is uniquely valuable for interview prep — candidates often know algorithms but don't know WHEN to use which one.
acceptanceCriteria: Advisor panel accessible via 'Help me choose' button
3-4 decision questions with multiple choice options
Based on answers, 1-3 algorithms are recommended with brief rationale
Recommended algorithms have 'Try it' button that selects and auto-runs
Advisor can be dismissed/collapsed
Decision logic covers sorting, graph, and DP categories
